title: Broken Access Control
description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures typically lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of data.
indicators: Missing authorization checks, IDOR (accessing resources by user-supplied ID without permission), CORS misconfiguration allowing unauthorized API access, privilege escalation, accessing admin pages as regular user.
prevention: Deny by default except for public resources. Implement access control mechanisms once and reuse. Enforce record ownership. Disable directory listing. Log access control failures and alert admins.

title: Cryptographic Failures
description: Failures related to cryptography which often lead to sensitive data exposure. Includes transmitting data in clear text, using old or weak cryptographic algorithms, and improper key management.
indicators: Data transmitted in plain text (HTTP, FTP), use of old or weak algorithms (MD5, SHA1, DES), hardcoded encryption keys, missing TLS, storing passwords in plain text or with reversible encryption.
prevention: Encrypt all sensitive data at rest and in transit using TLS. Use strong algorithms (AES-256, bcrypt, argon2). Never hardcode cryptographic keys. Disable caching for sensitive responses.

title: Injection
description: Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. Includes SQL injection, NoSQL injection, OS command injection, LDAP injection, and XSS.
indicators: String concatenation in SQL queries, use of eval(), exec(), system(), child_process.exec with user input, template literals in queries without parameterization, innerHTML with user data.
prevention: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Use ORMs. Validate and sanitize all user inputs. Use allowlists for input validation. Escape special characters in output.

title: Insecure Design
description: Flaws in the design and architecture of the application. Unlike implementation bugs, insecure design cannot be fixed by a perfect implementation — the design itself is flawed.
indicators: Missing rate limiting on sensitive operations, no CAPTCHA on login/registration, business logic that can be bypassed, lack of input validation at the design level, missing threat modeling.
prevention: Use secure design patterns. Establish a secure development lifecycle. Perform threat modeling. Write unit and integration tests for security-critical flows. Use reference architectures.

title: Security Misconfiguration
description: Improperly configured security settings. Includes unnecessary features enabled, default accounts/passwords, overly informative error handling, and missing security hardening.
indicators: DEBUG=true in production, CORS set to *, default admin credentials, verbose error messages exposing stack traces, unnecessary HTTP methods enabled, missing security headers.
prevention: Implement a repeatable hardening process. Remove unused features and frameworks. Review and update configurations. Use automated tools to verify configuration. Implement proper error handling.

title: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
description: Using components (libraries, frameworks) with known vulnerabilities. Includes not knowing versions of components used and not scanning for vulnerabilities regularly.
indicators: Outdated npm/pip/maven packages, deprecated libraries, known CVEs in dependencies, not running npm audit or equivalent, using unmaintained packages.
prevention: Remove unused dependencies. Monitor versions continuously. Run npm audit / pip audit regularly. Only obtain components from official sources. Prefer signed packages.

title: Identification and Authentication Failures
description: Weaknesses in authentication mechanisms. Includes permitting weak passwords, credential stuffing, missing multi-factor authentication, and improper session management.
indicators: Plain text password storage, weak hashing (MD5, SHA1 without salt), hardcoded credentials, session tokens in URLs, missing session timeout, no brute-force protection.
prevention: Use bcrypt/argon2 for password hashing. Implement multi-factor authentication. Do not ship with default credentials. Implement rate limiting on login attempts. Use secure session management.

title: Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Failures related to code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations. Includes insecure deserialization, using untrusted plugins or CDNs without integrity verification.
indicators: JSON.parse on untrusted input, eval() for deserialization, pickle.loads in Python, loading scripts from CDN without SRI hashes, auto-update without signature verification.
prevention: Use digital signatures to verify software/data integrity. Ensure libraries are from trusted repositories. Use SRI for CDN resources. Do not deserialize untrusted data. Review CI/CD pipeline security.

title: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Without logging and monitoring, security breaches cannot be detected. Insufficient logging, missing alerting, and lack of incident response planning.
indicators: No logging of login attempts, access control failures, or input validation failures. Logs stored only locally. No alerting or monitoring. Console.log of sensitive data in production.
prevention: Log all login, access control, and server-side input validation failures. Ensure logs have sufficient context for forensics. Use centralized log management. Establish incident response plan.

title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: SSRF occurs when a web application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. Allows attackers to make the server send requests to internal resources.
indicators: User-supplied URLs passed to HTTP clients (fetch, axios, http.get), accessing internal metadata endpoints, URL redirects without validation, importing data from user-provided URLs.
prevention: Sanitize and validate all client-supplied URLs. Enforce URL schemas, ports, and destinations with allowlists. Do not send raw responses to clients. Disable HTTP redirections.

title: Path Traversal
description: Improper limitation of a pathname to a restricted directory. Attackers can use ../ sequences to break out of the intended directory and access arbitrary files on the server.
indicators: fs.readFile/fs.readFileSync with user input in the path, path.join with unsanitized user input, '../' sequences not being filtered, serving files based on user-supplied filenames.
prevention: Validate and sanitize file paths. Use path.basename() to strip directory components. Use path.resolve() and verify the result is within the expected directory. Use chroot or containers.

title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
description: Injection of malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Includes reflected XSS, stored XSS, and DOM-based XSS. Leads to session hijacking, defacement, and malware distribution.
indicators: innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write() with user data, dangerouslySetInnerHTML in React, unescaped template rendering (EJS without <%- %>), v-html in Vue without sanitization.
prevention: Escape output encoding appropriate to the context (HTML, JS, URL, CSS). Use textContent instead of innerHTML. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP). Use sanitization libraries like DOMPurify.
